Chapter 7
Anaesthesia and analgesia
Angela M. Lennox and Bob Doneley
Analgesia Regardless of the type of pain, as a prey species, 
guinea pigs have only two basic responses to it: ‘fight or 
Pain management is complicated by the difficulty of 
flight’ (escape) and ‘freeze’ (conservation-withdrawal).
measuring pain in many exotic pets, particularly in prey 
Fight or flight responses are more common when the 
species. Pain management strategies are utilized for all 
animal is suffering from sudden or unexpected pain from 
surgical procedures, and to manage acute and chronic 
which it attempts to escape. These responses include:
pain due to disease or injury.
■  Excessive vocalization 
Pain assessment
■  Unexpected aggression (e.g. nipping at hands).
Consideration of anaesthesia for surgical procedures must 
include pain assessment, which is challenging in the guinea  Freeze responses, in contrast, are seen when the 
pig, as it is in many prey species. Objective scales for pain  guinea pig is in chronic or overwhelming pain (which is 
recognition have been developed for rabbits, rats and mice  perhaps more commonly seen in clinical practice). It may 
but there is less information available regarding guinea  be an attempt to minimize further pain that struggling 
pigs. Guinea pigs, like all vertebrates, appear to experience  would induce, and to avoid attracting the attention of 
different types of pain – acute or chronic; somatic, visceral  potential predators. These responses include:
or neuropathic pain.
Acute pain is associated with sudden tissue damage  ■  Immobility
and is of short duration, but it gradually resolves as the  ■  Absence or reduction of vocalization
injured tissues heal. It is relatively sharper and more severe  ■  Closed, dull or unfocused eyes
than chronic pain. ■  Inappetence
Chronic pain is any pain that lasts beyond the normal  ■  Reduced/absent faecal production
time expected for tissues to heal. It may become progres- ■  Piloerection
sively worse and reoccur intermittently and may have no  ■  Decreased grooming
apparent biological cause. It includes pain such as that  ■  Hunched posture.
associated with arthritis.
The following pain types can be either acute or  In addition to the above clinical signs of generalized 
chronic:  pain, localized pain may be reflected in chewing, hair pull-
ing or other self-mutilation behaviours over or in the vicinity

skin, bones, joints, tendons and muscles Care must be taken not to interpret lack of movement

Chapter 7 • Anaesthesia and analgesia
found more consistent differences in behaviour in guinea  Examples of analgesics and anaesthetics used in

2017). Remote observation was also more consistent than 
Local anaesthesia
measurement of food consumption. Combination analgesia 
appeared most effective. Inexpensive wireless home 
The precise mechanism of action of local anaesthetics 
monitoring systems can be adapted in the clinic to facilitate 
is unclear, but it is likely that they bind to sodium-selective 
remote postsurgical monitoring (Figure 7.1).
ionic channels in nerves, inhibiting the sodium permeabil-
ity that underlies action potential and depolarization of 
the cell membrane. This reversible interference with the 
generation and conduction of action potentials along the 
nerve reduces the number and frequency of impulses, 
thereby reducing nociceptor sensitization and minimizing 
central sensitization. When local anaesthetics are used 
pre-emptively, the number and frequency of impulses 
are reduced, thereby reducing nociceptor sensitization, 
which has the beneficial effect of minimizing central 
sensitization.
The length of time a drug blocks sensory transmission 
depends on how well it penetrates the axoplasm and 
reaches the sodium channel, how strongly it binds, and 
how quickly it is removed. Increasing lipid solubility pro-
motes penetration of the drug to the site of action, while 
the duration of the drug at the site of action is a function 
of the placement of the drug, the concentration used, the 
size of the molecule, perfusion at the site and the subse-
quent removal of the drug into the systemic circulation. 
Sensory nerve fibres are blocked before motor nerve 
fibres, meaning that pain and temperature sensation are 
A small plastic ‘igloo’ fitted with a simple inexpensive 
7.1 camera transmitting remotely to a tablet or smartphone  blocked first, followed by proprioception and motor func-
for postoperative observation of a guinea pig. tion. It is desirable that motor function and proprioception 
return to normal before local anaesthetics cease to block

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Sedation as an alternative to general 
anaesthesia
It should be kept in mind that not all procedures require 
general anaesthesia, and sedation should be consid-
ered in any situation where general anaesthesia is not 
absolutely indicated. Examples include for venipunc-
ture, positioning for radiographs, treatment of minor 
wounds and removal of small masses, as long as 
discomfort is carefully managed. The use of local 
analgesics is described in detail above (see ‘Local 
anaesthesia’). All agents described for pre-anaesthe-
sia (see ‘Premedication’, below) can be used for seda-
An intravenous cephalic catheter in a guinea pig. For this 
7.8
tion in the guinea pig. patient, induction was performed with slow intravenous

Fluid support: Fluid support should ideally be provided for 
all anaesthetic events. Ideal fluid rates for canine and feline 
surgical patients have been proposed; traditional sources 
recommend 10 ml/kg/h in animals without evidence of 
cardiac failure. In 2013, the American Animal Hospital 
Association (AAHA) published recommendations of 5 mg/
kg/h for dogs and 3 mg/kg/h for cats (Davis et al., 2013). 
The ideal surgical fluid rate for guinea pigs is unknown. The 
author [AML] uses an initial fluid rate of 8 ml/kg/h for most 
Forced mask ventilation in a guinea pig using a tight-
7.10
fitting mask over the nares and mouth. The seal must be 
complete for effective ventilation. There is an intraosseous 
catheter in the left tibia.
Intubation is challenging and often time-consuming in 
guinea pigs due to patient size and the presence of the 
palatial ostium, a unique round fold of tissue rostral to the 
glottis that limits access to the glottis. Epiglottal airway 
devices are not available for this species at the time of 
(a)
writing. The author [AML] does not routinely intubate 
guinea pigs except in cases where a face mask or nasal 
mask would interfere with specific surgical procedures 
(e.g. for surgery of the nasal cavity itself).
In dogs and cats, intubation provides protection of the 
airway from introduction of fluids or foreign material into 
the oral cavity. However, as guinea pigs are obligate nasal 
breathers, intubation requires retraction of the soft palate 
above the glottis, which in theory may increase the risk of 
introducing oral fluids into the airway along the endotra-
cheal tube, especially with the use of non-cuffed tubes, 
which are currently recommended for guinea pigs and 
rabbits in order to prevent tracheal mucosal injury.
(b)

smaller patients include obstruction due to mucus accu- (a) Demonstration of endotracheal intubation in a guinea
